coredata.dc:title: A high performance supercapacitor electrode materials based on carbon quantum dots originated from bamboo leaves of Southeast Sulawesi doped with nanomagnetite derived from iron sands

coredata.dc:description: This study evaluates the synthesis and characterisation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) originated from bamboo leaves of Southeast Sulawesi doped with nanomagnetite (Fe3O4) derived from iron sands as a high performance supercapacitor electrode materials. CQDs from bamboo leaves were successfully synthesised using hydrothermal method with the aid of acid oxidation and ultrasonic. Fe3O4 is obtained from the extraction of iron sand using ultrasonic waves combined with sodium hydroxide. The synthesised CQDs were then doped with Fe3O4 through a calcination method assisted by ultrasonic and hydrothermal processes to form Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites. The UV–Vis characterisation of CQDs from bamboo leaves showed a band gap value of 3.2 eV. The FTIR spectra showed peaks at 609 and 1635 cm−1 corresponding to F–O vibrations and CO stretching vibrations that characterise Fe3O4 and CQDs. In addition, XRD characterisation results showed the cubic structure of Fe3O4 and the amorphous structure of CQDs, TEM characterisation showed that the Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposite had a uniform size distribution with an average diameter of 57.49 nm. Electrochemical testing via CV showed that the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode had a larger curve area and higher specific capacitance than single Fe3O4. From the GCD data, the maximum specific capacitance of Fe3O4/CQDs electrode was recorded as 261.61 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g, with maximum energy density and power density of 36.67 Wh/kg and 997.66 W/kg, respectively. The EIS analysis results show that the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode has low diffusion and charge transfer resistance of 1.29 and 0.12 Ohm. These findings suggest that combining CQDs from bamboo leaves with Fe3O4 as a nanocomposite material can improve the performance of supercapacitor electrodes, making it a promising material for efficient and environmentally friendly energy storage applications.

head.ce:title: A high performance supercapacitor electrode materials based on carbon quantum dots originated from bamboo leaves of Southeast Sulawesi doped with nanomagnetite derived from iron sands

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study evaluates the synthesis and characterisation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) originated from bamboo leaves of Southeast Sulawesi doped with nanomagnetite (Fe3O4) derived from iron sands as a high performance supercapacitor electrode materials. CQDs from bamboo leaves were successfully synthesised using hydrothermal method with the aid of acid oxidation and ultrasonic. Fe3O4 is obtained from the extraction of iron sand using ultrasonic waves combined with sodium hydroxide. The synthesised CQDs were then doped with Fe3O4 through a calcination method assisted by ultrasonic and hydrothermal processes to form Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites. The UV–Vis characterisation of CQDs from bamboo leaves showed a band gap value of 3.2 eV. The FTIR spectra showed peaks at 609 and 1635 cm−1 corresponding to F–O vibrations and CO stretching vibrations that characterise Fe3O4 and CQDs. In addition, XRD characterisation results showed the cubic structure of Fe3O4 and the amorphous structure of CQDs, TEM characterisation showed that the Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposite had a uniform size distribution with an average diameter of 57.49 nm. Electrochemical testing via CV showed that the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode had a larger curve area and higher specific capacitance than single Fe3O4. From the GCD data, the maximum specific capacitance of Fe3O4/CQDs electrode was recorded as 261.61 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g, with maximum energy density and power density of 36.67 Wh/kg and 997.66 W/kg, respectively. The EIS analysis results show that the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode has low diffusion and charge transfer resistance of 1.29 and 0.12 Ohm. These findings suggest that combining CQDs from bamboo leaves with Fe3O4 as a nanocomposite material can improve the performance of supercapacitor electrodes, making it a promising material for efficient and environmentally friendly energy storage applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth of the global population and advancements in technology have led to a significant increase in energy consumption, resulting in severe environmental impacts due to the predominant use of fossil fuels as the primary source of energy [1]. The use of fossil fuels and their contribution to climate change can be effectively reduced through the implementation of three different strategies: (a) enhancing the efficiency of conventional power conversion devices/systems through waste heat recovery; (b) developing efficient and environmentally friendly energy conversion devices, such as fuel cells; and finally, (c) transitioning to renewable energy sources derived from nature with minimal adverse environmental impacts [2]. Researchers have highlighted the challenges nations face in increasing capacity factors while maintaining a balance between utilising renewable energy sources and fossil fuels [3]. According to the IEA (International Energy Agency), the electricity industry is expected to experience the fastest growth in renewable energy sources, thus preparing for a sustainable future. As the electricity industry is recognised as a significant contributor to global climate change, there are numerous discussions regarding the high utilisation of renewable energy sources for global electricity production [4].

The sustainability of current and future electrical networks requires a net-zero strategy that can store excess energy generation in real-time environments [5]. Optimal coordination of Energy Storage Systems (ESS) significantly enhances power reliability and resilience, especially in the deployment of Renewable Energy Sources (RES) [6]. The most popular ESS used in this context are Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and supercapacitors (SC) [7]. Mature and well-developed rechargeable battery chemistry has led to widespread applications in various fields, including electric/hybrid vehicles, smartphones, medical equipment, laptops, and small to large-scale energy storage applications. However, rechargeable batteries have many drawbacks, such as lower power density, shorter cycle life, longer charging time, thermal management issues, and environmental safety concerns [8].

Supercapacitors (SC) are efficient alternatives to batteries in energy storage as they do not need to be recharged before use and can store higher power levels [9]. SCs can store and release energy quickly due to reversible ion adsorption at the electrode/electrolyte interface, unlike conventional batteries that rely on chemical reactions for energy storage and release [10]. The charge storage capacity in supercapacitors theoretically originates from two capacitive behaviours: one related to the double-layer structure at the electrode/electrolyte interface and the other to pseudo-capacitance [11–14]. Capacitance and charge storage are two criteria dependent on the type of electrode material used in supercapacitors. According to the energy density formula, the specific capacitance of the electrode material and the potential difference between positive and negative electrodes determine the energy density of the capacitor [15]. The production of environmentally friendly and low-cost active electrode materials, large surface area, better conductivity, and higher electrochemical stability should be carefully focused on when selecting active materials on the electrode surface [16].

As electrode materials govern the electrochemical characteristics, transition metal oxides (TMOs) that are electrochemically active and exhibit effective pseudocapacitive behaviour have gained significant attention as electrode materials for supercapacitors [17]. Among them, Fe3O4 possesses different valence states (Fe2+ ↔ Fe3+ + e), high theoretical specific capacitance (2299 F/g), large potential window, low cost, high specific surface area (approximately 179 m2/g) [18]. However, as a pseudocapacitive material, Fe3O4 has several drawbacks, such as high contact resistance, lower conductivity, reduced accessible electrochemical surface area, and short cycle life, which degrade the capacitive performance of the material [19]. Combination with conductive carbon and nanostructure construction is two effective strategies to modify the electrode capacitance of Fe3O4 by enhancing the electronic conductivity of the electrode and electrolyte access to the electrode [20].

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have attracted much interest due to their non-toxicity, excellent consistency, high surface-to-volume ratio, tunable photoluminescence, and biocompatibility for various electrode modifications [21]. CQDs perform enhancers for pseudocapacitive materials such as transition metal sulfides, conductive polymers, and transition metal oxides [22]. CQDs are zero-dimensional nano-sized carbon cores, t ypically less than 10 nm in size with quasi-spherical shapes, possessing multiple functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amine [23]. CQDs consist of sp2/sp3 conjugated core, can donate uniform dispersion, excellent electron transfer/reservoir properties, structural and compositional properties, and more potent quantum edge effects [24]. CQDs' outstanding quantum size effect and conductive properties can be converted into fluorescence emission, making them suitable for electronic applications [25]. The large π-conjugated electronic structure and size-dependent quantum confinement of CQDs can be applied to energy storage devices [26].

CQDs are used in various applications, but unfavourable quantum yields of CQDs limit their applications. Improvements in the optical and electronic transfer properties of CQDs are warranted. This issue is addressed through surface passivation, functionalisation, and doping with metals or non-metals [27]. Nanotechnology is a methodology aimed at enhancing specific properties of target materials. Due to the high surface-to-volume ratio, nanostructured materials exhibit distinguishable features compared to their bulk forms [28]. Nanomaterials are usually combined with other functional materials/nanomaterials, both inorganic and organic, to form nanocomposites for practical applications [29]. Nanocomposites are nanocomposites where one phase has nano-scale morphology, such as nanoparticles, nanotubes, or nano-flat structures [30]. Nanocomposites are materials with nano-scale components (10−9 m) [31].

Conversion of CQDs from biomass waste is considered an environmentally friendly synthesis pathway due to its low cost, sustainability, environmental friendliness and commercialisation [32]. Previous research and development found bamboo leaves are a good carbon source and can be made into composites [33]. Therefore, in this study, bamboo leaves were used as raw materials for the synthesis of CQDs because bamboo leaves are organic waste materials that can be recycled and are widely available in the environment. Meanwhile, Fe3O4 was obtained from Tapunggaya iron sand, Southeast Sulawesi, Indonesia. According to Nurdin et al., (2016), Tapunggaya iron sand has 12 elements with Fe, Ti, Mg and Si as the four highest elements. Fe3O4 is generally obtained using expensive commercial Fe sources, such as iron salts; FeCl3, FeCl2, FeSO4, Fe(NO3)3, Fe(ClO4)3.6H2O. Iron complexes; [Fe(CO)5], [Fe{N-(SiMe3)22] and La0.5Sr0.5FeO3. For this reason, efforts to obtain materials containing magnetic materials continue, especially in finding sources of Fe available in the area with more suitable and economical synthesis methods [34]. The utilisation of natural resources such as bamboo leaves and iron sand has not been done efficiently by people in Southeast Sulawesi, Indonesia.

Bamboo leaves were taken in the courtyard area of the Halu Oleo University campus, Kendari, Southeast Sulawesi, Indonesia. While iron sand was taken from Tapunggaya beach, Konawe Utara, Sulawesi Tenggara, Indonesia. Glycerol (E Merck), sodium hydroxide (E Merck), sulfuric acid (E Merck), whatman filter paper, 0.22 μm membrane filter. Deionised water (WaterOne-Onemed), polyvinylidene fluoride (HSV900/SOLVAY 5130), N-methyl pyrrolidone (Merck 8.06072), carbon black super P (CAS number: 1333-86-4), nickel foam (Purity: 99.99 % and Porosity: 96 %), potassium hydroxide (CAS Number 1310-58-3) were purchased from AMI Scientific, Bandung Barat, Jawa Barat, Indonesia. All chemicals were received and used without any purification.

CQDs were synthesised using hydrothermal method with the aid of acid oxidation and ultrasonic process. Initially, 2 g of bamboo leaf powder were mixed with a sulfuric acid solution (8 mL), deionised water (5 mL) was added, and vigorously stirred. Then, deionised water (50 mL) was added for sonication at 40 °C for 1 h. Next, the solution was stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 1 h. Then, it was filtered using whatman filter paper. The formed residue was washed thoroughly with deionised water until a neutral pH was achieved. Subsequently, the residue was dissolved in deionised water (30 mL) for sonication at 40 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the suspension was transferred to a Teflon autoclave, heating at 120 °C for 24 h. Then, the suspension was sonicated again at 40 °C for 1 h. Afterwards, it was centrifuged (3000 rpm) for 45 min and filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Finally, a supernatant liquid containing CQDs was obtained. The supernatant liquid of CQDs was then irradiated with 365 nm UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of magnetite (Fe3O4) from iron sand
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 extraction is carried out by ultrasonic method in combination with an alkaline solution (sodium hydroxide). The process begins by attracting magnetic material to the iron sand using an external magnet. Then crushed using a mortar and pestle. Next, it was sieved using a 200 mesh sieve and washed thoroughly using deionised water to remove contaminants and small non-magnetic materials. Then in the oven at 95 °C for 5 h 20 g of magnetic material was reacted with 50 mL of 8 M sodium hydroxide solution to be sonicated with a frequency of 60 Hz at 40 °C for 2 h. After that, the suspension was separated between filtrate and residue using an external magnet, the residue obtained was washed until the pH was neutral and baked at 95 °C for 5 h. So that black coloured material is obtained as an initial validation of the formation of Fe3O4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs doped with Fe3O4 (nanocomposites Fe3O4/CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The supernatant liquid (CQDs) of 15 mL was added to 5 mL of glycerol and 10 mL of deionised water to form a homogeneous solution, and then 0.092 g of Fe3O4 was added. Next, it was sonicated at a frequency of 60 Hz with a temperature of 40 °C for 1 h. It was then transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, it was sonicated again at 40 °C with a frequency of 60 Hz for 1 h. The obtained sample was calcined at 300 °C for 4 h and designated as Fe3O4 doped CQDs material.

Characterisation of CQDs using a Hitachi U-2900 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer was carried out to determine the absorption pattern formed so as to obtain the absorption edge used to determine the optical properties and band gap value of CQDs. Fourier Transform Infra Red (FTIR) type IRPrestige-21 SHIMADZU and ATR IR Nicolet is 5, to analyse the presence of functional groups from bamboo leaves, Fe3O4, Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites and CQDs. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) type Bruker D8 Advance Eco was used to analyse the crystalline phase and degree of crystallinity of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites, then adjusted to the International Centre for Diffraction Data (ICDD). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) type Jeol JEM-1400, to determine the surface morphology of Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites and to determine the particle size of CQDs and Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites.

The electroactive materials consisting of each (pristine Fe3O4 or Fe3O4/CQDs), super P carbon black, and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) were mixed at mass ratio of 8:1:1. Subsequently, the mixture was milled for 15 min and heated in oven at 60 °C. Then, it was dissolved using 25 mL of N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP). Next, the mixture was deposited onto 1 × 1 cm2 nickel foam using the drop-casting method. It was then dried using a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 12 h. Finally, each working electrode was signed by a pristine Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposite. Herein, each the electrode has mass loading i.e., 4 mg.

Electrochemical tests using Bipotentiostat CS2350, Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) with Galvanostatic Charge and Discharge (GCD) were performed to determine the capacitive characteristics of the electrodes which have pseudocapacitance, diffusion rate behaviour, specific capacitance, power density and energy density. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) was performed to determine the diffusion resistance and charge transfer behaviour. Measurements were carried out in 2 M potassium hydroxide solution as a conducting medium, so that the analyte is not affected by the difference in potential changes given quickly, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, Pt wire as the opposing electrode and pristine Fe3O4 or Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposite as the working electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs characterisation using a UV–Vis Spectrophotometer
ce:section.ce:para: Absorbance measurements were conducted within the wavelength range of 200 nm–800 nm. As depicted in Fig. 1 a exhibits a peak at a wavelength of 370 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π∗ transition of the CO group in the sp2 aromatic system due to the oxidation of carbon-based nanoparticles [35]. According to Ref. [36] the absorption centred at 370 nm is assigned to the n-π∗ chemical bond transition at the edge of the graphitised carbon core (i.e., CO at the edge state), which is generally the main emission site responsible for the bright fluorescence of CQDs. Meanwhile, wavelength absorption up to 800 nm can be attributed to the presence of functional groups on the surface or interconnected polymer chains [37].

The formation of functional groups containing oxygen within the structure of CQDs can lead to excitation-dependent fluorescent emissions [26]. Exposure to UV irradiation contributes to blue photoluminescence, as indicated in Fig. 1a, demonstrating a more significant quantum confinement effect in smaller-sized CQDs [38]. Additionally, the appearance of blue-fluorescing CQDs under UV light can be explained by fewer surface defects, which can reduce non-radiative electron transitions and result in more emitted electrons [39].

As observed in (Fig. 2 ), the FTIR spectrum of bamboo leaves exhibits peaks at 3448, 2924, 1633, and 1103 cm−1, which can be attributed to OH stretching vibrations, CH stretching vibrations, CO stretching vibrations, and bending vibrations of the C–OH group, respectively. On the other hand, the FTIR spectrum of CQDs shows significant peaks at 3321, 1633, and 659 cm−1, which can be associated with OH stretching vibrations, CO stretching vibrations, and C–H bending vibrations. The wavenumber at 1633 cm−1 indicates the characterisation of CQDs sp2hybridisation from the C chain, consistent with the research conducted by Ref. [42]. According to (Wang et al., 2015), the specific peak range and functional groups in bamboo leaves are located in a broad peak with a range of 3395. cm−1 assigned to hydroxyl stretching vibrations, absorption at 2937 cm−1 caused by CH stretching vibrations, peaks at 1614–1647 cm−1 representing CO stretching vibrations of the –COH group, and absorption range at 1115 cm−1 related to the bending vibration of the C–OH group.

The FTIR spectrum obtained from magnetite (Fe3O4) exhibits significant peaks at 601 cm−1, which can be associated with the vibration mode of the metal-oxygen (Fe–O) bond in the crystal lattice of Fe3O4. It is consistent with the research conducted by Ref. [43], where the vibration mode of the metal-oxygen (Fe–O) bond in the crystal lattice of Fe3O4 ranges from 580 to 631 cm−1. The FTIR spectrum of the Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposite reveals peaks at 609, 1118, 1635, 2931, and 3363 cm−1, which can be associated with the vibration of Fe–O, bending vibration of the C–OH group, stretching vibration of CO, stretching vibration of CH, and stretching vibration of OH, respectively. The characteristic wave numbers corresponding to functional groups of the CQDs, Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposite were summarized in Table 1 .

Fig. 3 shows the X-ray diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites. Diffraction peaks on single Fe3O4 were formed at angles 2θ = 18.32°, 30.25°, 35.47°, 43.23°, 53.56°, 57.13° and 62.65° with miller indices of (111), (220), (311), (400) (422), (511) and (440) respectively. The diffraction peaks formed show similarity with the ICDD database No. 01-075-0033, Fe3O4 has a cubic phase. On the other hand, the diffraction pattern of the Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposite shows a characteristic carbon peak at an angle of 2θ = 16° with a miller index (002) showing graphite characteristics that identify CQDs as amorphous, the same results obtained by Sargin et al. (2019). Formed with a fairly low diffraction peak indicates the composition of CQDs in the sample is relatively low [44]. According to the research results of Liu et al. (2014), it shows that CQDs have an amorphous structure with a peak range of 10°–35° in the hkl (002) plane. The degree of crystallinity obtained from Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites is 70 % and 50.1 %. Fig. 3 shows that there are changing in the peaks of Fe3O4/CQDs sample from the pristine Fe3O4. Due to CQDs have low crystallinity [13], loading CQDs on Fe3O4 causes decreasing degrees of crystallinity of the Fe3O4. In addition, this assumes that this is due to limited crystalline mass in the nanocomposite sample, which is also strongly influenced by the other ingredient. This changing suggests that Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites have successfully been produced. In addition, formation of the nanocomposites was implied by shifting some wave number on FTIR spectra of Fe3O4 before and after adding CQDs (Fig. 2).

The synthesised nanocomposite shows a typical pomegranate-like shape according to TEM images. This shape has similarities with that obtained by Zhang et al. (2020). Fig. 4 a shows unagglomerated CQDs with the results of particle size distribution analysis using ImageJ having an average diameter size of 3.25 nm, shown in Fig. 4b. Fig. 4c shows Fe3O4 and CQDs agglomerate to form a nanocomposite which indicates CQDs are dispersed on the surface of Fe3O4, this is similar to the results of research conducted by Latifa et al. Fig. 4d shows the size distribution of Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites with an average diameter of 57.49 nm. In the synthesis process, the mixture consists of many particles due to heating, carbon nanoparticles are connected to each other to form agglomerated particles [45]. The universal approach to analysing TEM with ImageJ follows the steps that have been done [46].

Fig. 5 a presents the voltammogram profiles of pristine Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CQDs electrodes recorded in a 1 M ethylene carbonate solution at a scan rate of 50 mV/s, with a potential range from −1.05 to −0.1 V for pristine Fe3O4 and from −1.05 V to −0.05 V for Fe3O4/CQDs. Both materials exhibit similar voltammogram shapes, but it can be observed that the voltammogram shape generated from Fe3O4/CQDs is broader compared to that of pristine Fe3O4. The cyclic areas of the voltammograms for Fe3O4/CQDs and pristine Fe3O4 are 7.047 C/g and 4.716 C/g, respectively It suggests that the doping process of Fe3O4 into CQDs has successfully increased the electronic conductivity of Fe3O4 material, where existing CQDs can facilitate electron transfer during charge-discharge cycles, resulting in an enormous current response compared to pristine Fe3O4. Additionally, the tiny size of CQDs can increase the effective surface area of the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode. This increased surface area can enhance the number of reactive sites available for electrochemical reactions between the electrolyte solution and the working electrode [47]. The CV curve of Fe3O4 does not exhibit clear redox peaks, unlike the curve of Fe3O4/CQDs, which shows slight oxidation peaks around −0.76 V and reduction peaks around −0.78 V. This observation may be attributed to the electrolyte solution being unable to oxidise Fe3O4 when its active sites are not fully accessible to the electrolyte. However, with the presence of CQDs agglomerating Fe3O4, more significant active sites are provided compared to pristine Fe3O4, thereby facilitating reversible electron transfer processes on the electrode surface between Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions (Fe3+ + e− ↔ Fe2+) [48]. From the shape of the CV curve, it is evident that the curve of the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode resembles a skewed rectangle with reversible redox peaks. Coupled with the preceding explanation of the underlying phenomena of this pattern, this ultimately indicates the characteristic voltammogram of a supercapacitor electrode exhibiting behaviour patterns akin to pseudocapacitive properties [49].

Fig. 5b shows the voltammogram curves of Fe3O4/CQDs electrode at various scan rates ranging from 5 mV/s to 100 mV/s. The voltammogram curves exhibit an increase in curve area with increasing scan rate. However, from the voltammogram curve, redox peaks are not clearly indicated at low scan rates (5–10 mV/s), suggesting potential kinetic hindrance limiting the redox reaction. In this scenario, the diffusion rate of electroactive species to the electrode surface becomes a determining factor [50]. The limited number of species reaching the electrode surface at low potentials prevents the precise initiation of redox peaks on the voltammogram [51].

Conversely, at high scan rates (20–100 mV/s), the contribution of reaction kinetics increases, allowing the electrochemical response on the electrode surface to become more dominant, resulting in observable redox peaks [52]. However, the role of diffusion remains significant, as evidenced by the relatively broad shape of the formed peaks. Existing diffusion rates may restrict the electrochemical response if electroactive species cannot rapidly reach the electrode surface to participate in the reaction. Additional factors, such as the adsorption of electroactive species on the electrode surface and electrode polarisation, can influence the electrochemical response at different scan rates [53]. Therefore, the variability in the voltammogram as a function of scan rate emerges due to the complex interaction between reaction kinetics, diffusion, and electrode surface processes.

To further comprehend the characteristics of Fe3O4/CQDs in its application as a supercapacitor electrode, we also subsequently determined several critical parameters from the observed CV curves, including the nanocomposites capacitance, power density, and energy density. Within the testing system employing three electrodes, we determined the specific capacitance value using the following equation [54];

Here, Csp is specific capacitance (F/g), E is energy density (Wh/kg), P is power density (W/kg), A is a cyclic area of voltammogram (C/g), m is the actively loaded material mass (g), s is used for the scan rate (V/s), and ΔV is the voltage window (V).

After calculating the area of the cycle formed from the voltammogram curve at various scan rates, the specific capacitance values were subsequently obtained as 160.41, 132.12, 110.35, 99.16, 94.2, 87.83, 78.95, and 74.88 F/g at scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 80, and 100 mV/s, respectively, for pristine Fe3O4 material. Meanwhile, the specific capacitance values for the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode were sequentially obtained as 248.62, 212.39,164.94, 146.85, 123.62, 119.19, 102.40, and 99.45 F/g at the same scan rates. These results can be seen in Fig. 5c, which shows the relationship between specific capacitance and scan rate. As depicted in the graph, at the same scan rate, the specific capacitance values generated from the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode tend to be higher than those obtained from pristine Fe3O4. However, when examining the trend lines formed as the scan rate increases, the linearity of pristine Fe3O4 tends to be more stable compared to the linearity produced by the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode. It may be due to the concentration polarisation around the electrode surface, which increases when the electrochemical reaction requires a rapid ion supply to maintain high capacitance levels [55,56]. In this case, although CQDs can enhance the porosity of Fe3O4, ultimately increasing its capacitance value, CQDs have not yet been able to improve the stability of Fe3O4 in its ability to retain ions under higher voltage conditions. Under such conditions, the electrode's ability to accept ions from the electrolyte decreases, resulting in a decline in capacitance value.

Meanwhile, in the determination of energy density and power density values of the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode, the results were successively obtained as 30.90, 26.40, 20.50, 18.25, 15.37, 14.81, 12.73, and 12.36 Wh/kg for energy density, and 556.24, 950.37, 1476.03, 1971.22, 2212.61, 2666.56, 3665.63, and 4450.04 W/kg for power density. Fig. 5d presents the Ragone plot, which shows the relationship between power density and energy density obtained from the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode. The Ragone plot results indicate that the range of values from the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode is quite suitable for application as a supercapacitor electrode [57,58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of GCD (Galvanostatic Charge and Discharge)
ce:section.ce:para: To further strengthen the CV measurement results, a GCD analysis must be performed to compare it with the previous CV analysis. Fig. 6 a presents GCD graph data from pristine Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CQDs electrode. The figure represents the charge and discharge process of the working electrode with current over the testing period. It can be observed that the curve shape of pristine Fe3O4 exhibits an almost perfect triangular shape with slight curvature at the end of the discharge cycle. This curve indicates the charge-discharge cycles of an EDLC (Electric double-layer capacitor).

In contrast, the curve shape generated by the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode tends to have a GCD curve shape resembling that of a supercapacitor with pseudo-capacitance characteristics [24,58,59]. Fig. 6b shows the GCD measurement results at various current density values. It can be seen that the increase in current density values causes a significant decrease in GCD results from 1 A/g to 2 A/g. However, this decrease slows down after surpassing the current density value of 2 A/g up to 3 A/g. The specific capacitance from the GCD curve in the configuration of the three-electrode cell can be obtained with the following equation [54,60];
(8)

As can be seen from Fig. 6c, the capacitance values obtained from pristine Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CQDs electrode are 155 and 296 F/g, respectively, at a current density of 1 A/g, showing an almost twofold increase in capacitance value. The specific capacitance values of the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode were calculated to be 261.61, 153.64, and 131.47 F/g at current densities of 1, 2, and 3 A/g, respectively. The corresponding energy and power density values can be further determined from these capacitance values. The energy densities obtained from the calculations are 36.67, 22.22, and 20 Wh/kg, with power densities of 997.66, 1991.72, and 2989.24 W/kg, respectively. The relationship between these values can be seen in Fig. 6d. For validation purposes, the results of CV and GCD analysis of Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites can be seen in Table 2 .

According to the results in Table 2, The Nyquist plot indicates that the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode has a low bulk electrolyte resistance (Rbe), calculated from the intersection point with the x-axis at high frequencies, and also has a low charge transfer resistance (Rct), calculated from the span of the single semicircle along the x-axis from high to low frequencies [61–63]. Additionally, the Fe3O4/CQDs path at low frequencies shows a steep slope toward the imaginary resistance, indicating an increased dominance of the capacitive behaviour in the electrode, thus resulting in lower diffusion resistance [64]. The EIS results on the Nyquist plot show that Fe3O4/CQDs have low diffusion and charge transfer resistance of 1.29 and 0.12 Ohm, which is beneficial for electron transfer and ion diffusion, thereby demonstrating good electrochemical behaviour with specific capacity and rate performance [65].

This study successfully synthesised and characterised carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from bamboo leaves doped with magnetite (Fe3O4) and evaluated their application as a high performance supercapacitor electrode material. From the research findings, several key points can be concluded; CQDs from bamboo leaves were successfully synthesised using hydrothermal method with the aid of acid oxidation and ultrasonic. Fe3O4 is obtained from the extraction of iron sand using ultrasonic waves combined with sodium hydroxide. The synthesised CQDs were then doped with Fe3O4 through a calcination method assisted by ultrasonic and hydrothermal processes to form Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposites. The UV–Vis characterisation of CQDs from bamboo leaves showed a band gap value of 3.2 eV. The FTIR spectra showed peaks at 609 and 1635 cm-1 corresponding to F–O vibrations and CO stretching vibrations that characterise Fe3O4 and CQDs. In addition, XRD characterisation results showed the cubic structure of Fe3O4 and the amorphous structure of CQDs, TEM characterisation showed that the Fe3O4/CQDs nanocomposite had a uniform size distribution with an average diameter of 57.49 nm. Electrochemical testing via CV showed that the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode had a larger curve area and higher specific capacitance than single Fe3O4. From the GCD data, the maximum specific capacitance of Fe3O4/CQDs electrode was recorded as 261.61 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g, with maximum energy density and power density of 36.67 Wh/kg and 997.66 W/kg, respectively. The EIS analysis results show that the Fe3O4/CQDs electrode has low diffusion and charge transfer resistance of 1.29 and 0.12 Ohm. The combination of CQDs with Fe3O4 in the form of nanocomposites enhanced the performance of supercapacitor electrodes, making them a promising candidate for energy storage applications in the form of supercapacitors. Therefore, this study demonstrates that bamboo leaves, as an abundant and environmentally friendly biomass source, can be processed into effective CQDs, which can be doped with Fe3O4 to achieve better electrochemical activity than pristine Fe3O4 in supercapacitor applications.
